questions.question: Which organization developed H.323, and what was its telecom lineage?
options: ITU, adapted from ISDN Q.931 signaling
questions.explanation: H.323 was developed by the ITU (telecom world) by adapting ISDN Q.931 call signaling for packet-switched IP networks.

questions.question: What are the three main H.323 protocol phases for call setup?
options: RAS (admission), Q.931 (call signaling), H.245 (control + media channel setup)
questions.explanation: H.323 uses H.225 RAS for gatekeeper admission, H.225 Q.931 for call signaling, and H.245 for capability exchange and logical channel opening.

questions.question: Why did SIP eventually displace H.323 in most VoIP deployments?
options: SIP uses a single text-based protocol that is simpler to implement, debug, and extend than H.323's multiple binary protocols
questions.explanation: H.323 uses ASN.1 binary encoding across multiple protocols (Q.931, H.245, RAS), making it complex. SIP's single text-based protocol is simpler to implement and debug, leading to wider adoption.

questions.question: What is the role of an H.323 gatekeeper?
options: It provides address translation, admission control, and bandwidth management for H.323 terminals
questions.explanation: The gatekeeper is an optional H.323 entity that manages terminals: it translates aliases to addresses, controls call admission (ARQ/ACF), and manages bandwidth allocation.
